mnemonics.title: VIRTUAL Memory Aid
mnemonics.content:
V - Virtual table (vtable)
I - Inheritance hierarchy determines call
R - Runtime resolution
T - This pointer guides dispatch
U - Using vptr to lookup
A - Actual object type wins
L - Late binding mechanism

mnemonics.title: PURE Virtual Remember
mnemonics.content:
PURE = Pure functions Require
U - Unimplemented (no body)
R - Runtime dispatch (polymorphic)
E - Enforce derived implementation

= 0 means 'no implementation here' - forces derived to provide it!

mnemonics.title: Diamond Problem Solution
mnemonics.content:
VIRTUAL Inheritance:
V - Virtual inheritance creates single instance
I - Intermediate classes use 'virtual' keyword
R - Resolves ambiguity automatically
T - Top (most derived) controls initialization
U - Unique base class instance
A - Avoids duplicate base subobject
L - Levels of inheritance properly handled

acronyms.usage: Lookup table containing function pointers for virtual function dispatch at runtime
Hidden pointer in each object pointing to class vtable for virtual function resolution
Mechanism to identify actual type of object at runtime using typeid operator
Compiler-generated field for virtual base class displacement in constructors/destructors
Objects of derived class should be usable wherever base class objects are expected

visualTips.visualization: Draw: Object → vptr → vtable → Function Pointer → Actual Function. Show how object type determines which function runs, not pointer type.
Stack hierarchy diagram: Base → Derived1 → Derived2. Arrow shows: 'Call goes to BOTTOM-most implementation in hierarchy'.
Diamond shape: A (top) → B and C (sides) → D (bottom). Virtual inheritance removes duplicate A, shows single shared A.
Show box (Derived) being copied into smaller box (Base) - everything outside Base box is 'cut off' (sliced away).
Draw table with columns: Function Index | Pointer to Function. Each class has its own vtable. vptr points to it.

analogies.analogy: Like a chain of command - when a message comes, it goes to the most senior person (most derived) in the hierarchy who can handle it, not just who the message was addressed to.

Each class has a menu (vtable) of virtual function options. When you order (call function), the actual dish served depends on which restaurant (object) you're at, not which menu (pointer type) you showed.
Pure virtual function is like a job requirement - it says 'someone MUST provide this capability' without specifying how. Derived classes must fulfill the contract.
Virtual destructor is like having a cleanup crew that comes AFTER the main event. Without virtual, only the basic cleanup runs, leaving derived class resources uncleaned.
Slicing is like making a black-and-white photocopy of a color document - you lose all the color (derived class) information, only the basic structure (base class) remains.
